title: Transformations
blurb: Translations, reflections, rotations, dilations; symmetry; and congruence and similarity re-seen through motion.
buildsOn: Congruence (Unit 4) and similarity (Unit 7); coordinates from Grade 6.

lessons.title: Translations (Slides)
lessons.concreteComparison: Slide a book straight across a table without turning it — every corner moves the same distance in the same direction, and the book stays exactly the same size and shape. That is a **translation**: a slide.
lessons.objective: Translate a figure by a vector, writing the rule (x, y) → (x + a, y + b), and see that a slide preserves size and shape.
concept: A **transformation** takes a figure (the **preimage**) to a new figure (the **image**). We mark image points with a prime: $A \to A'$ (read "A prime").
A **translation** slides every point the same distance in the same direction — described by a **vector** $\langle a, b \rangle$: $a$ right, $b$ up (negatives go left / down).
In coordinates, a translation is $(x, y) \to (x + a, y + b)$. Add the vector to every vertex.
A translation is a **rigid motion**: the image is **congruent** to the preimage — same lengths, same angles, just moved.
lessons.keyIdea: A translation slides every point by the same vector ⟨a, b⟩: (x, y) → (x + a, y + b). It is a rigid motion, so the image is congruent to the original.

facts.title: Translation rule
facts.explanation: Add the vector to each vertex.

workedExamples.statement: Translate the point $(2, 3)$ by the vector $\langle 4, 1 \rangle$.
Translate $(5, -2)$ left $3$ and up $6$ — the vector $\langle -3, 6 \rangle$.

commonMistakes.text: Swapping the two components of the vector.
commonMistakes.correction: The **first** number moves you horizontally (x), the **second** vertically (y): $\langle a, b \rangle$ means $+a$ to x, $+b$ to y.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking a slide changes the figure's size.
commonMistakes.correction: A translation is **rigid** — every length and angle is unchanged. The image is **congruent**.

steps.title: Every point moves the same way
beats: A **transformation** moves a figure to a new position.
The starting figure is the **preimage**; the result is the **image**.
A **translation** is a **slide** — same distance, same direction, for every point.
The figure keeps its size and shape.

steps.title: A slide by a vector
steps.teach: Tap **Apply** to slide triangle $ABC$ by the vector $\langle 3, 2 \rangle$ — 3 right and 2 up. Watch every vertex move the same way, and read how each coordinate changes. The image $A'B'C'$ is the same size and shape.

steps.title: Add the vector
beats: A vector $\langle a, b \rangle$ means: $a$ right, $b$ up.
The rule is $(x, y) \to (x + a,\ y + b)$.
Add $a$ to every x-coordinate, $b$ to every y-coordinate.
Negative $a$ slides left; negative $b$ slides down.

steps.title: Translating a point
steps.intro: Add the vector to the point.

steps.title: Translation practice
steps.intro: Add the vector to each coordinate.

problems.prompt: The vector $\langle a, b \rangle$ moves a point —
problems.explanation: First component is horizontal, second vertical.

steps.title: Slides can go any direction
steps.teach: Here the vector is $\langle -4, 1 \rangle$ — 4 left and 1 up. Negatives just reverse the direction. Every vertex still moves identically, so the image stays congruent.

steps.title: Size and shape survive
beats: A translation is a **rigid motion** — a slide that moves without distorting.
Lengths, angles, and area are all unchanged.
The image is **congruent** to the preimage: $ABC \cong A'B'C'$.
Only the position changes.

steps.title: Translating a figure
steps.intro: Slide every vertex by the same vector.

steps.title: Figure practice
steps.intro: Every vertex uses the same vector.

problems.prompt: A translation maps a triangle to one that is —
problems.explanation: Rigid motion: same size and shape.

steps.title: Wallpaper and honeycombs
steps.body: A pattern that repeats by sliding — wallpaper, floor tiles, a honeycomb — is built entirely from translations of one tile. Mathematicians proved there are exactly 17 essentially different ways to fill the plane with a repeating pattern, and translations are the engine behind every one of them.

steps.title: Translate a point
steps.prompt: What is the image of $(3, -1)$ under the translation $\langle 2, 5 \rangle$?

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **translation** slides every point by a vector $\langle a, b \rangle$: $(x, y) \to (x+a,\ y+b)$.
The **first** component moves x (horizontal), the **second** moves y (vertical).
It is a **rigid motion** — the image is **congruent** to the preimage.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's slide points and figures by vectors.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: The image of a point is written —

steps.title: You've finished "Translations (Slides)"
steps.body: You can slide any figure by a vector. Next: reflections — flipping a figure across a line.

lessons.title: Reflections (Flips)
lessons.concreteComparison: Hold a shape up to a mirror and you see it flipped — left and right swap, but it's the same size. A **reflection** flips a figure across a line, the **line of reflection**, which acts exactly like that mirror.
lessons.objective: Reflect a figure over the x-axis, the y-axis, and the line y = x, using the coordinate rules, and see that a flip is a rigid motion.
concept: A **reflection** flips a figure across a **line of reflection**. Each point and its image are the same distance from that line, on opposite sides.
Over the **x-axis**: $(x, y) \to (x, -y)$ — the y-coordinate flips sign.
Over the **y-axis**: $(x, y) \to (-x, y)$ — the x-coordinate flips sign.
Over the line **y = x**: $(x, y) \to (y, x)$ — the two coordinates swap. A reflection is a **rigid motion**, so the image is **congruent**.
lessons.keyIdea: A reflection flips a figure across a line. Over the x-axis (x, y)→(x, −y); over the y-axis (x, y)→(−x, y); over y = x (x, y)→(y, x). It is a rigid motion.

commonMistakes.correction: Over the **x-axis** you flip **y** (points move up/down across it); over the **y-axis** you flip **x**.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to swap for y = x.
commonMistakes.correction: Reflecting over $y = x$ **swaps** the coordinates: $(x, y) \to (y, x)$.

steps.title: Flip across a line
beats: A **reflection** flips a figure across a line — the **line of reflection**.
It works like a mirror: same size, but reversed.
Each point and its image are equally far from the line.
We'll flip over the x-axis, the y-axis, and the line $y = x$.

steps.title: Flip over the x-axis
steps.teach: Tap **Apply** to flip triangle $ABC$ over the **x-axis** (the bold horizontal line acts as the mirror). Each point keeps its x but its y flips sign: $(x, y) \to (x, -y)$.

steps.title: Negate the y-coordinate
beats: The x-axis is the mirror.
A point above it lands the same distance below.
Rule: $(x, y) \to (x, -y)$.

problems.prompt: Reflecting over the x-axis flips the sign of —

steps.title: Flip over the y-axis
steps.teach: Now the **y-axis** is the mirror. Each point keeps its y but its x flips sign: $(x, y) \to (-x, y)$. Tap **Apply**.

steps.title: Negate the x-coordinate
beats: The y-axis is the mirror.
Left and right swap; up/down stays.
Rule: $(x, y) \to (-x, y)$.

steps.title: Flip over the line y = x
steps.teach: The mirror can be a slanted line. Over $y = x$ (the blue diagonal), the coordinates **swap**: $(x, y) \to (y, x)$. A point already on the line doesn't move.

steps.title: Swap the coordinates
beats: The line $y = x$ runs diagonally through the origin.
Reflecting over it **swaps** x and y.
Rule: $(x, y) \to (y, x)$.

steps.title: Your face isn't symmetric
steps.body: A reflection over a vertical line is what a mirror does — which is why your mirror image parts its hair on the 'wrong' side. Photos of a face made by mirroring one half onto the other look subtly wrong to us: no real face is perfectly symmetric, and our brains notice the too-perfect flip.

steps.title: Reflect a point
steps.prompt: What is the image of $(5, 2)$ reflected over the x-axis?

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **reflection** flips a figure across a line; it is a **rigid motion** (congruent image).
Over the **x-axis**: $(x, y) \to (x, -y)$. Over the **y-axis**: $(x, y) \to (-x, y)$.
Over **y = x**: $(x, y) \to (y, x)$ — swap the coordinates.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's reflect points over the x-axis, the y-axis, and y = x.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: A reflection produces an image that is —

steps.title: You've finished "Reflections (Flips)"
steps.body: You can flip a figure over any of the three key lines. Next: rotations — turning a figure about a point.

lessons.title: Rotations (Turns)
lessons.concreteComparison: The hands of a clock turn about the center pin; a Ferris wheel turns about its hub. A **rotation** turns a figure about a fixed point — the **center of rotation** — by a certain angle, without changing its size.
lessons.objective: Rotate a figure 90°, 180°, and 270° about the origin using the coordinate rules, and see that a turn is a rigid motion.
concept: A **rotation** turns a figure about a fixed **center of rotation** by an angle. We measure angles **counterclockwise** unless told otherwise.
About the **origin**, the quarter-turn rules are: $90°: (x, y) \to (-y, x)$; $180°: (x, y) \to (-x, -y)$; $270°: (x, y) \to (y, -x)$.
A $180°$ turn sends every point straight through the origin to the opposite side — the same as reflecting through the origin.
A rotation is a **rigid motion**: the image is **congruent** to the preimage.
lessons.keyIdea: A rotation about the origin: 90° → (−y, x); 180° → (−x, −y); 270° → (y, −x). It is a rigid motion, so the image is congruent.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to swap x and y for a 90° turn.
commonMistakes.correction: A $90°$ turn **swaps** the coordinates and negates the new first one: $(x, y) \to (-y, x)$.

commonMistakes.text: Only flipping one sign for a 180° turn.

steps.title: Turn about a point
beats: A **rotation** turns a figure about a fixed **center**.
We'll always turn about the **origin**.
Angles are measured **counterclockwise**.
The size and shape never change.

steps.title: A quarter turn (90°)
steps.teach: Tap **Apply** to turn triangle $ABC$ by $90°$ counterclockwise about the origin (the blue dot). The rule is $(x, y) \to (-y, x)$ — swap, then negate the new first coordinate.

steps.title: Swap and negate
beats: A $90°$ counterclockwise turn: $(x, y) \to (-y, x)$.
Swap the two coordinates, then negate the first.
Four $90°$ turns bring you all the way around.

problems.prompt: Rotations are measured — unless stated otherwise.

steps.title: A half turn (180°)
steps.teach: A $180°$ turn sends each point straight through the center to the opposite side: $(x, y) \to (-x, -y)$. Both signs flip. Tap **Apply**.

steps.title: Flip both signs
beats: Every point goes through the origin to the far side.
It's the same forwards and backwards — clockwise or counter.

steps.title: Why S, N and Z look the same upside down
steps.body: Rotate the letters S, N, Z, H, I, O, or X by $180°$ and they land back on themselves. That's why the word 'SWIMS' reads the same rotated a half-turn, and why some playing cards look identical no matter which end is up.

steps.title: Rotate a point
steps.prompt: What is the image of $(5, 2)$ rotated $90°$ about the origin?

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **rotation** turns a figure about a center; about the origin the rules are $90°: (-y, x)$, $180°: (-x, -y)$, $270°: (y, -x)$.
Angles are **counterclockwise** unless stated otherwise.
It is a **rigid motion** — the image is **congruent**.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's rotate points 90°, 180°, and 270° about the origin.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: A rotation produces an image that is —

steps.title: You've finished "Rotations (Turns)"
steps.body: You can turn a figure about the origin. Next: symmetry — when a figure maps onto itself.

lessons.title: Symmetry
lessons.concreteComparison: A butterfly's two wings match across its body; a snowflake looks the same after a small turn. **Symmetry** is when a transformation maps a figure exactly onto itself — a reflection or a rotation that leaves the figure looking unchanged.
lessons.objective: Identify line (reflection) symmetry and rotational symmetry, count lines of symmetry, and give the order of rotational symmetry.
concept: A figure has **line symmetry** (reflection symmetry) if some line divides it so one half is the mirror image of the other. That line is a **line of symmetry**.
A figure can have several lines of symmetry: a rectangle has $2$, an equilateral triangle has $3$, and a square has $4$. A regular $n$-gon has $n$.
A figure has **rotational symmetry** if a rotation of less than $360°$ about its center maps it onto itself. The **order** is how many times it matches in one full turn.

A regular $n$-gon has rotational symmetry of **order $n$** (it matches every $360°/n$). A figure with order-$2$ rotational symmetry is said to have **point symmetry** (it maps onto itself under a $180°$ turn).
lessons.keyIdea: Line symmetry: a reflection maps the figure onto itself (count the mirror lines). Rotational symmetry: a turn under 360° maps it onto itself; the order is how many matches in a full turn. A regular n-gon has n of each.

workedExamples.statement: How many lines of symmetry does a square have?
workedExamples.solution: A square (regular $4$-gon) has $4$: two through opposite sides, two through opposite corners.

workedExamples.statement: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a regular hexagon?
workedExamples.solution: A regular $n$-gon has order $n$, so a hexagon has order $6$ (it matches every $360°/6 = 60°$).

commonMistakes.text: Counting a full 360° turn as rotational symmetry.
commonMistakes.correction: Every figure looks the same after $360°$. Rotational symmetry needs a match at **less than** $360°$.

commonMistakes.text: Assuming every figure with line symmetry has the same number of lines.
commonMistakes.correction: Count carefully: a rectangle has $2$ lines, not $4$ — its diagonals are **not** lines of symmetry.

tryIt.statement: How many lines of symmetry does an equilateral triangle have?
tryIt.solution: $3$ — one from each vertex through the opposite side.

tryIt.statement: A regular pentagon matches every how many degrees when rotated?

steps.title: Mapping a figure onto itself
beats: **Symmetry** = a transformation that leaves a figure looking unchanged.
**Line symmetry**: a reflection maps the figure onto itself.
**Rotational symmetry**: a turn (under $360°$) maps it onto itself.
We'll count lines of symmetry and orders of rotation.

steps.title: Line symmetry
steps.teach: This triangle is symmetric about the **y-axis**. Tap **Apply** to reflect it over the y-axis — the image lands exactly on the original. That overlap IS the symmetry: the y-axis is a line of symmetry.

steps.title: One half mirrors the other
beats: A **line of symmetry** splits a figure into mirror-image halves.
Reflecting across it maps the figure onto itself.
A rectangle has $2$; an equilateral triangle has $3$.
A square has $4$; a regular $n$-gon has $n$.

steps: Two through opposite sides, two through opposite corners.
Only through the midpoints of opposite sides.
$2$ (the diagonals are NOT lines of symmetry).

problems.prompt: An equilateral triangle has how many lines of symmetry?
A regular hexagon has how many lines of symmetry?

problems.explanation: Reflecting a non-square rectangle over a diagonal does not map it onto itself.

steps.title: Rotational symmetry
steps.teach: This parallelogram maps onto itself under a $180°$ turn about the origin. Tap **Apply** — the image lands back on the original. That's rotational symmetry of order $2$ (point symmetry).

steps.title: Matches within one turn
beats: A figure has **rotational symmetry** if a turn under $360°$ maps it onto itself.
The **order** = how many times it matches in a full turn.
It matches every $360° \div \text{order}$.
Order $2$ (a $180°$ match) is called **point symmetry**.

problems.prompt: A square has rotational symmetry of order —
A regular pentagon matches every — degrees.
'Point symmetry' means a figure maps onto itself under a — turn.

steps.title: Snowflakes and the number six
steps.body: Every snowflake has six-fold symmetry — order-6 rotational symmetry and six lines of symmetry — because water molecules lock into a hexagonal lattice as they freeze. No two snowflakes are identical, yet every one obeys the same symmetry, matching itself every $60°$.

steps.title: Order of symmetry
steps.prompt: A regular octagon (8 sides) has rotational symmetry of what order?
steps.explanation: A regular $n$-gon has order $n$, so order $8$ (matches every $360/8 = 45°$).

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Line symmetry**: a reflection maps the figure onto itself — count the mirror lines.
**Rotational symmetry**: a turn under $360°$ maps it onto itself; the **order** is matches per full turn.
A regular $n$-gon has $n$ lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry of order $n$.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's count lines of symmetry and orders of rotation.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Lines of symmetry of a square?
Lines of symmetry of a rectangle (not square)?
Order of rotational symmetry of an equilateral triangle?
A regular decagon (10 sides) matches every — degrees.
'Point symmetry' is rotational symmetry of order —
How many lines of symmetry does a regular octagon have?

steps.title: You've finished "Symmetry"
steps.body: You can spot both kinds of symmetry. Next: dilations — the one transformation that changes size.

lessons.title: Dilations (Resizing)
lessons.concreteComparison: A projector throws a small slide onto a big screen — same picture, scaled up. Zooming a photo does the same. A **dilation** resizes a figure by a **scale factor** from a center point, keeping its shape but changing its size.
lessons.objective: Dilate a figure from the origin by a scale factor k, using (x, y) → (kx, ky), and see that the image is similar (not congruent) unless k = 1.
concept: A **dilation** resizes a figure from a **center** by a **scale factor** $k$. From the origin, the rule is $(x, y) \to (kx, ky)$ — multiply both coordinates by $k$.
If $k > 1$ the figure **enlarges**; if $0 < k < 1$ it **reduces**; if $k = 1$ it is unchanged.
A dilation is **NOT** a rigid motion (unless $k = 1$): lengths scale by $k$, so the image is **similar** to the preimage — same shape, different size — with all angles preserved.
This is the similarity of Unit 7 seen as a motion: every image side is $k$ times the matching original side and parallel to it.
lessons.keyIdea: A dilation from the origin multiplies both coordinates by the scale factor k: (x, y) → (kx, ky). k > 1 enlarges, 0 < k < 1 reduces. The image is similar to the original (angles kept, lengths ×k).

workedExamples.statement: Dilate $(3, 2)$ from the origin by scale factor $k = 2$.

commonMistakes.text: Adding the scale factor instead of multiplying.
commonMistakes.correction: A dilation **multiplies**: $(x, y) \to (kx, ky)$. Adding would be a translation, not a resize.
Unless $k = 1$, a dilation changes size, so the image is **similar**, not congruent.

steps.title: Resize from a center
beats: A **dilation** changes a figure's **size** but not its shape.
It works from a **center** by a **scale factor** $k$.
We'll dilate from the **origin**.
This is similarity (Unit 7) seen as a motion.

steps.title: An enlargement (k = 2)
steps.teach: Tap **Apply** to dilate triangle $ABC$ from the origin by $k = 2$. Each vertex moves to twice its distance from the origin along the ray from O: $(x, y) \to (2x, 2y)$. The image is twice as big — similar, not congruent.

steps.title: Multiply by the scale factor
beats: From the origin: $(x, y) \to (kx,\ ky)$.
Multiply BOTH coordinates by $k$.
$k > 1$ enlarges; $0 < k < 1$ reduces.

problems.prompt: A dilation with $k = 2$ makes the figure —

steps.title: A reduction (k = ½)
steps.teach: With $0 < k < 1$ the figure shrinks toward the center. Here $k = \tfrac12$: each vertex moves to half its distance from O. Tap **Apply**.

steps.title: Shape kept, size changed
beats: A dilation (with $k \ne 1$) is NOT a rigid motion.
Every length is multiplied by $k$; angles are unchanged.
So the image is **similar** to the preimage: $ABC \sim A'B'C'$.
Image sides are $k \times$ the originals and parallel to them.

examples.prompt: A side of length $5$ is dilated by $k = 3$. New length?

problems.prompt: A dilation (with $k \ne 1$) makes an image that is —
A side of length $12$ dilated by $k = \tfrac13$ becomes —

steps.title: Scale models and blueprints
steps.body: An architect's blueprint is a dilation of a real building with a tiny scale factor — say $k = \tfrac{1}{100}$ — and a movie's scale-model spaceship is a dilation the other way. Because dilations keep every angle and proportion, the small copy tells you exactly what the full-size version will look like.

steps.title: Dilate a point
steps.prompt: What is the image of $(5, 3)$ dilated from the origin by $k = 2$?

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **dilation** from the origin: $(x, y) \to (kx,\ ky)$ — multiply both coordinates by $k$.
$k > 1$ **enlarges**, $0 < k < 1$ **reduces**, $k = 1$ leaves it unchanged.
The image is **similar** (not congruent): angles kept, lengths $\times k$.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's dilate points from the origin and track the scale factor.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: A dilation with $k \ne 1$ produces an image that is —
A length of $9$ dilated by $k = \tfrac13$ becomes —
The rule for a dilation from the origin is —

steps.title: You've finished "Dilations (Resizing)"
steps.body: You can resize a figure by any scale factor. Next: combining transformations — and how they explain congruence and similarity.

lessons.title: Compositions, Congruence & Similarity
lessons.concreteComparison: You can slide a figure, then flip it, then turn it — one move after another. A sequence of transformations is a **composition**. Doing several rigid motions in a row is how we PROVE two figures are congruent; adding a dilation is how we prove they're similar.
lessons.objective: Apply a composition of two transformations in order, and connect rigid motions to congruence and dilations to similarity.
concept: A **composition** applies one transformation, then another, to the result. Order matters: do the **first** transformation, then apply the **second** to its image.
A composition of **rigid motions** (slides, flips, turns) is itself a rigid motion — the final image is **congruent** to the original.
Two figures are **congruent** exactly when a sequence of rigid motions maps one onto the other. This is the modern definition of congruence, matching Unit 4.
Two figures are **similar** exactly when a sequence of rigid motions **and a dilation** maps one onto the other — same shape, possibly different size (Unit 7).
lessons.keyIdea: A composition does one transformation then another (order matters). Rigid motions compose to a rigid motion → the figures are congruent. Allowing a dilation too → the figures are similar.

facts.title: Congruent
facts.latex: rigid motions map one to the other

workedExamples.statement: Translate $(2, 3)$ by $\langle 1, 1 \rangle$, then reflect the result over the x-axis. Final image?
Reflect $(4, 1)$ over the y-axis, then rotate $180°$ about the origin. Final image?

commonMistakes.text: Doing the transformations in the wrong order.
commonMistakes.correction: Apply the **first** transformation, then the **second** to that image. Reversing the order can change the result.

commonMistakes.text: Calling figures related by a dilation 'congruent'.
commonMistakes.correction: A dilation changes size, so figures related by it are **similar**. Congruence needs rigid motions only.

tryIt.statement: Translate $(1, 2)$ by $\langle 3, 0 \rangle$, then reflect over the x-axis.
Two triangles are related by a translation and a reflection only. Congruent or similar?

steps.title: One move after another
beats: A **composition** does several transformations in sequence.
Apply the **first**, then the **second** to its image.
**Order matters** — the result can depend on it.
This ties transformations back to congruence and similarity.

steps.title: Step 1 of a composition
steps.teach: We'll translate triangle $ABC$ by $\langle 2, 1 \rangle$ first. Tap **Apply** to see this first image — then imagine flipping THAT over the x-axis for step 2.

steps.title: First, then second
beats: Do the first transformation to the preimage.
Then apply the second to the resulting image.
Example: translate $(2,3)$ by $\langle 1,1 \rangle \to (3,4)$.
Then reflect over the x-axis $\to (3, -4)$.

examples.prompt: $(2, 3)$: translate $\langle 1, 1 \rangle$, then reflect over the x-axis.
$(4, 1)$: reflect over the y-axis, then rotate $180°$.

problems.prompt: $(1, 2)$: translate $\langle 3, 0 \rangle$, then reflect over the x-axis?
$(3, 5)$: reflect over the x-axis, then translate $\langle 0, 2 \rangle$?
In a composition you apply the — transformation first.
problems.explanation: Then the second acts on that image.

steps.title: Same size and shape
beats: Slides, flips, and turns are **rigid motions**.
Any composition of them is still rigid.
So the final image is **congruent** to the original.
Two figures are **congruent** ⟺ rigid motions map one onto the other.

steps.title: Add a dilation → similarity
steps.teach: A dilation changes size. If a sequence includes one, the figures are **similar**, not congruent. Here the triangle is dilated by $k = 2$: same shape, twice the size. Tap **Apply**.

steps.title: Same shape, scaled size
beats: Allow a **dilation** in the sequence and size can change.
Then the image is **similar** to the original, not congruent.
Two figures are **similar** ⟺ rigid motions AND a dilation map one onto the other.
This is exactly the similarity of Unit 7.

steps.title: Congruent or similar?
steps.intro: Rigid only → congruent; with a dilation → similar.

problems.prompt: Figures related by a rotation and a translation only are —
Figures related by a reflection then a dilation ($k=3$) are —
Every rigid motion produces an image that is — to the original.

steps.title: Congruence, redefined by motion
steps.body: For centuries 'congruent' meant 'same measurements.' Modern geometry flips it around: two figures are congruent precisely when one can be slid, flipped, and turned onto the other. It's the same idea a child uses stacking shape blocks — and it makes every congruence proof a story about motion.

steps.title: Compose two moves
steps.prompt: Translate $(2, 2)$ by $\langle 3, 1 \rangle$, then reflect over the x-axis. What is the final image?

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **composition** does one transformation then another; **order matters**.
A composition of **rigid motions** is rigid → the figures are **congruent**.
Allowing a **dilation** too → the figures are **similar** (same shape, scaled size).

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's compose transformations and classify congruent vs similar.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: $(2, 3)$: translate $\langle 1, 1 \rangle$, then reflect over the x-axis?
$(4, 1)$: reflect over the y-axis, then rotate $180°$?
Figures related by rigid motions only are —
problems.explanation: Rigid motions keep size and shape, so the figures are congruent.

problems.prompt: Figures related by a dilation (and rigid motions) are —
In a composition, changing the order can —

steps.title: You've finished "Transformations"
steps.body: You can slide, flip, turn, resize, and combine — and you can say exactly when figures are congruent or similar. That completes transformations. One unit left: Coordinate Geometry, the course capstone.

lessons.title: Transformation Matrices
lessons.concreteComparison: You've been writing transformations as coordinate rules: "$(x, y) \to (-y, x)$". A matrix packs that rule into a $2\times 2$ grid of numbers, $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$, that MULTIPLIES points. Same rotation, new notation — but now transformations compose by multiplication, and one number inside (the determinant) reports exactly how areas scale.
lessons.objective: Apply a 2×2 matrix to a point, recognize the standard rotation/reflection/scaling matrices, and use the determinant as the area scale factor.
concept: A point becomes a column $\binom{x}{y}$, and a matrix acts by row-times-column: $$\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}\binom{x}{y} = \binom{ax + by}{cx + dy}.$$ Every rotation about the origin, reflection through an axis, and scaling from Unit 12 is one of these.

The standard cast, worth recognizing on sight: rotation $90°$ counter-clockwise $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$; rotation $180°$ $\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$; reflection over the $x$-axis $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$, over the $y$-axis $\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$, over $y = x$ $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$; scaling by $k$ $\begin{pmatrix} k & 0 \\ 0 & k \end{pmatrix}$.

A fast identification trick: the matrix's COLUMNS are the images of $(1, 0)$ and $(0, 1)$. Ask where the transformation sends those two points, stand the answers up as columns — done. That's also how you decode an unfamiliar matrix: read its columns.

The **determinant** $ad - bc$ is the transformation's area report: any shape's area gets multiplied by $|ad - bc|$. Rotations and reflections have determinant $\pm 1$ (rigid — areas untouched); a scaling by $k$ has determinant $k^2$. A negative sign means orientation flipped — the plane got mirrored.
lessons.keyIdea: Matrix × column point = image; columns are the images of (1,0) and (0,1); |det| = the area scale factor.

facts.title: Matrix acts on a point
facts.explanation: Row times column, twice — the whole mechanism.

facts.title: Columns tell all
facts.explanation: To build or decode a matrix, track just the two unit points.

facts.title: Determinant = area factor
facts.explanation: ±1 for rigid motions; k² for scaling by k; negative sign = orientation flip.

workedExamples.statement: Apply $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ to the point $(3, 1)$ and name the transformation.
workedExamples.solution: $\binom{0 \cdot 3 + (-1) \cdot 1}{1 \cdot 3 + 0 \cdot 1} = \binom{-1}{3}$. The columns are the images of $(1,0) \to (0,1)$ and $(0,1) \to (-1,0)$: a $90°$ counter-clockwise rotation.

workedExamples.statement: Which matrix reflects the plane over the line $y = x$? Apply it to $(5, -2)$.
workedExamples.solution: Reflection over $y = x$ swaps coordinates, sending $(1,0) \to (0,1)$ and $(0,1) \to (1,0)$; stand those up as columns: $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. On $(5, -2)$: $\binom{0 \cdot 5 + 1 \cdot (-2)}{1 \cdot 5 + 0 \cdot (-2)} = \binom{-2}{5}$.

workedExamples.statement: The matrix $\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ is applied to a figure of area $5$. Find the image's area.
workedExamples.solution: Determinant: $3 \cdot 2 - 0 \cdot 0 = 6$. Areas scale by $|6|$: the image has area $6 \cdot 5 = 30$.

commonMistakes.correction: The point is a COLUMN on the right: top entry = first ROW · point, bottom entry = second ROW · point. Writing the point as a row silently transposes the transformation.
Area scales by |det|. A determinant of −1 keeps area exactly — the sign only says orientation flipped (a reflection happened).

tryIt.statement: Apply $\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$ to $(4, -7)$ and name the transformation.
tryIt.solution: $(-4, 7)$ — every point flips through the origin: a $180°$ rotation.

tryIt.statement: A dilation uses $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$. Where does $(3, 5)$ land, and by what factor do areas grow?
tryIt.solution: $(6, 10)$; the determinant is $4$, so areas quadruple — scale factor $2$ squares on the way to area.

steps.title: Rules are wordy; matrices are compact
steps.body: Unit 12 wrote every transformation as a coordinate rule: $(x,y) \to (-y, x)$ and friends. The exam's favorite upgrade packs the rule into a $2 \times 2$ **matrix** that multiplies points. Nothing new happens to the plane — but the notation composes, computes, and carries an area report inside.

steps.title: The turn you already know
steps.teach: A $90°$ counter-clockwise rotation sends $(1, 0) \to (0, 1)$ and $(0, 1) \to (-1, 0)$. Stand those two images up as columns and you've BUILT the rotation's matrix: $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. Every matrix in this lesson is decoded the same way — columns are the images of the two unit points.

steps.title: Row times column, twice
steps.body: $$\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}\binom{x}{y} = \binom{ax + by}{cx + dy}$$ The standard cast: rotations $90°$ $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ and $180°$ $\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$; reflections over the axes $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$, $\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ and over $y=x$ $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$; scaling $\begin{pmatrix} k & 0 \\ 0 & k \end{pmatrix}$.
beats: point = column; matrix multiplies from the left
columns = images of (1,0) and (0,1)
det = ad − bc reports the area factor

steps.title: One multiplication
steps.explanation: Top: $1 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 3 = 2$. Bottom: $0 \cdot 2 + (-1) \cdot 3 = -3$. The $y$-coordinate flips: reflection over the $x$-axis.

steps.title: The area report
steps.explanation: $\det = 0 \cdot 0 - (-1) \cdot 1 = 1$. Rotations are rigid: every area survives untouched, and orientation is preserved.

steps.title: Decode by columns, always
steps.body: Handed an unfamiliar matrix? Its first column is where $(1,0)$ goes, its second is where $(0,1)$ goes. Sketch those two arrows and the transformation names itself — faster and safer than memorizing every matrix in the zoo.

steps.title: Your screen does this constantly
steps.body: Every frame of a 3-D game multiplies millions of points by transformation matrices — rotations, scalings, and projections chained by matrix multiplication. GPUs are, at heart, machines for doing this lesson billions of times per second.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Matrix × column point: top = row 1 · point, bottom = row 2 · point.
Columns are the images of $(1,0)$ and $(0,1)$ — build and decode with them.
$|\det| = ad - bc$ (absolute value) scales every area; rigid motions have $\det = \pm 1$.

practice.statement: The translation $(x, y) \to (x + 3, y - 5)$ is applied to $(2, 4)$. Find the image.
Reflect $(3, -7)$ across the $x$-axis, and then reflect the original point across the $y$-axis.
practice.solution: Across the $x$-axis, $y$ flips: $(3, 7)$. Across the $y$-axis, $x$ flips: $(-3, -7)$.
Reflection across $y = x$ swaps the coordinates: $(-2, 5)$.

practice.statement: Rotate $(4, 1)$ by $90°$ counterclockwise about the origin.
practice.solution: The $90°$ CCW rule is $(x, y) \to (-y, x)$: image $(-1, 4)$.

practice.statement: How many lines of symmetry does a regular pentagon have, and what is its smallest angle of rotational symmetry?
practice.solution: Five lines (one through each vertex), and rotational symmetry of order $5$: smallest turn $360 \div 5 = 72°$.

practice.statement: A dilation centered at the origin with $k = 2$ maps a triangle with vertices $(1, 2)$ and $(3, 0)$ (among others). Find those image vertices, and state what happens to the triangle's perimeter and area.
practice.solution: Images: $(2, 4)$ and $(6, 0)$. Perimeter doubles ($\times k$); area quadruples ($\times k^2 = 4$).

practice.statement: Apply the composition to $(2, 3)$: first translate by $(x + 4, y)$, then reflect across the $x$-axis. Name the composition.
practice.solution: Translate: $(6, 3)$. Reflect: $(6, -3)$. A translation followed by a reflection over a parallel-to-translation line is a glide reflection.

testYourself.statement: A translation maps $(-1, 4)$ to $(3, 2)$. Find the translation vector.
Rotate $(5, 0)$ by $90°$ counterclockwise about the origin.
How many lines of symmetry does a non-square rectangle have, and what is its rotational symmetry?
testYourself.solution: Two lines (through the midpoints of opposite sides — the diagonals do NOT work), and rotational symmetry of order $2$ ($180°$).

testYourself.statement: A dilation with $k = \tfrac{1}{3}$ maps $(9, -6)$ to what point? Is the image figure congruent or similar to the original?
testYourself.solution: $(3, -2)$. Similar but not congruent — dilations preserve shape (angles) but scale lengths by $k \ne 1$.

testYourself.statement: Which of these transformations always produce a figure CONGRUENT to the original: translation, reflection, rotation, dilation with $k = \tfrac{3}{4}$?
testYourself.solution: Translations, reflections, and rotations — the rigid motions — preserve all lengths. A dilation with $k = \tfrac{3}{4}$ shrinks lengths, so its image is similar, not congruent.
